About
About

A careful I Ching guide for English readers.

I Ching Reading Guide explains hexagrams, changing lines, relating patterns, and coin readings in plain English. The goal is to make the structure understandable without turning the tradition into dramatic prediction.

What the site is for

This site is built for readers who want a calm way to study or use the I Ching. Some visitors arrive after casting a hexagram somewhere else. Others use the coin reading tool here and want to understand the result. In both cases, the site focuses on practical interpretation: what the pattern names, what part of the situation is moving, and what kind of next step may fit the moment.

The pages are written for beginners, but they avoid treating beginners as careless. Instead of saying a hexagram simply means luck, success, failure, or danger, the guide explains timing, pressure, restraint, support, return, conflict, progress, and other themes that can be applied carefully to real decisions.

Editorial approach

Each guide is written as educational cultural content. The site uses traditional terms such as primary hexagram, changing line, and relating hexagram, then translates the idea into plain English. The wording is intentionally cautious. A reading may help a person reflect on a relationship, project, job decision, or personal question, but it should not be used as proof that a specific event must happen.

When the site discusses paid reports, the same boundary applies. A report can organize the user's question, cast result, and next-step notes. It does not provide medical, legal, financial, psychological, safety, or guaranteed prediction advice. Important choices should be checked against evidence and qualified support.

What will continue to improve

The current roadmap is to keep strengthening the 64-hexagram lookup, add more line-level explanations, improve the free tool, expand guide pages, and make paid reports easier to understand. The standard is simple: every page should help a reader make sense of the I Ching more clearly than before they arrived.

Useful starting points

Cast a hexagram
Browse all 64 hexagrams
Read beginner guides
Read the disclaimer

Site accountability

Editorial and support boundaries

The site keeps a clear difference between cultural explanation and professional advice. Cultural explanation helps readers understand terms, reading structure, symbolism, and responsible interpretation. Professional advice belongs to qualified people in fields such as medicine, law, finance, mental health, safety, and crisis support. This boundary is repeated across the site because it protects readers and keeps the content honest.

Feedback is welcome when a page is unclear, a link is broken, or a phrase sounds like an internal note rather than public-facing content. The site is being improved toward a fuller guide library and a stronger paid-report product, so reader questions and correction notes help identify weak pages before they become a trust problem.

Content quality

How pages are maintained

The site is maintained with three quality checks in mind. First, each page should be useful to a reader who arrives from search and has no prior context. Second, public wording should sound like reader-facing education, not internal development notes. Third, commercial pages should explain the product clearly without exaggerating what an I Ching report can provide.

When thin or unclear pages are found, the priority is to improve them rather than hide them. Core pages such as tools, guides, FAQ, reports, about, and contact need enough context to stand on their own. That standard is especially important before submitting the site for advertising review.

I Ching Reading Guide

A calm I Ching tool for hexagrams, changing lines, and reflective decisions.
